design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore pre-rendered environments, discover clues, solve logical puzzles to unlock access to new Ages, piece together backstory through environmental and textual discovery
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Mystery and uncertainty—players begin with minimal context, no explicit goals, and must infer objectives through observation and exploration
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Discover and explore a mysterious, visually stunning island world; uncover a hidden narrative through patient investigation and deductive reasoning
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects conventional adventure game conventions: no combat, no time pressure, no failure states, no handholding. Commits entirely to nonverbal, environmental storytelling and logical puzzle-solving as the primary engagement mechanism. Prioritizes immersion and contemplation over action or narrative exposition

difficulty.checkpointing: No explicit checkpoints; progress is saved through puzzle completion and Age access. No death mechanic—player cannot fail
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player controls exploration pace entirely. Optional 'Zip' feature allows rapid traversal of explored areas, but risks missing clues. No difficulty settings documented

progression.unlock_mechanism: Puzzle solutions on Myst Island unlock access to linking books, which transport player to four separate Ages (Selenitic, Stoneship, Mechanical, Channelwood). Each Age must be explored to gather clues and complete the primary puzzle on Myst
replayability_hooks: Multiple narrative branches based on player choice at game's end (whom to aid)
Nonlinear exploration allows different puzzle-solving orders
Hidden clues and journal pages reward thorough exploration
Environmental storytelling reveals new context on replay

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through pre-rendered imagery and object placement
Journal pages and scattered text providing character backstory
Linking books containing detailed world descriptions
Age design reflecting character history and conflict (Sirrus vs. Achenar trapped in red/blue books)
Predominantly nonverbal narrative—player constructs understanding through exploration rather than exposition

faction_indicators: Red and blue books representing trapped sons Sirrus and Achenar—player must choose which to aid
Atrus as absent patriarch whose linking book technology and moral authority frame the conflict
Catherine (wife) implied as third party in family conflict
danger_signals: Linking books function as traps—innocuous-looking objects conceal danger
Island's isolation and player's involuntary transportation create entrapment atmosphere
Family conflict (Sirrus vs. Achenar) suggests internal corruption and betrayal
environmental_storytelling: Myst Island library as knowledge repository and gateway to other worlds
Each Age reflects its creator's character: Mechanical Age's complexity suggests Achenar's nature, Stoneship's decay suggests abandonment or destruction
Puzzle machinery and mechanisms as manifestations of Atrus's technological mastery
Objects scattered throughout reveal daily life interrupted
